examples.title: Basic Minimax for Tic-Tac-Toe
examples.description: Implements the basic Minimax algorithm for an unbeatable Tic-Tac-Toe AI without Alpha-Beta pruning. Demonstrates recursive evaluation of game states.
examples.explanation: 1. check_winner examines rows, columns, and diagonals for a winner or tie. 2. get_available_moves finds all empty cells. 3. make_move creates a new board state with the player's symbol. 4. minimax recursively evaluates all possible game states: if maximizing (AI's turn), it seeks highest score; if minimizing (opponent's turn), it seeks lowest score. 5. Base cases return scores: +1 for AI win, -1 for opponent win, 0 for tie. 6. Depth is used to prefer faster wins and slower losses. 7. get_best_move calls minimax for all available moves and selects the best one.
examples.timeComplexity: O(b^d) where b is branching factor (~9) and d is depth (~9)

examples.title: Minimax with Alpha-Beta Pruning for Connect Four
examples.description: Implements Minimax with Alpha-Beta pruning for Connect Four (7 columns x 6 rows). Demonstrates how pruning significantly reduces the search space while maintaining optimal results.
examples.explanation: 1. Alpha-beta pruning maintains two values: alpha (best value for maximizer) and beta (best value for minimizer). 2. When beta <= alpha, the current branch is cut as it won't affect the final decision. 3. The evaluation function score_position checks all possible 4-cell windows horizontally, vertically, and diagonally. 4. It awards points for having 3-4 pieces in a row with empty spaces, and penalizes opponent's advancing positions. 5. minimax function takes depth parameter to limit search depth (here 4), making it practical for real-time play. 6. The function returns (column, score) tuple - the best column to play and its evaluated score. 7. For maximizing player, we update alpha when finding better scores; for minimizing, we update beta. 8. Early termination occurs when: winning move found, no valid moves, or depth exhausted.
examples.timeComplexity: O(b^(d/2)) with alpha-beta pruning vs O(b^d) without, where b~7 and d=4
examples.spaceComplexity: O(d) for recursion stack plus O(b*d) for copying boards

examples.title: Advanced Minimax with Iterative Deepening and Position Ordering
examples.description: Advanced Connect Four implementation featuring iterative deepening, move ordering optimization, and heuristic center column preference for improved AI performance.
examples.explanation: 1. Iterative Deepening: The AI starts at depth 1 and progressively increases depth until time runs out, always returning the best move found so far. This ensures we have a valid move even if computation time is limited. 2. Move Ordering (order_moves): Prioritizes moves that lead to immediate wins (priority 0), then moves that block opponent wins (priority 1), then center columns (priority based on distance from center). This maximizes pruning effectiveness. 3. Center Column Preference: The score_position function gives +3 points per piece in the center column, as center control is strategically valuable in Connect Four. 4. Alpha-beta with proper ordering can achieve searching up to depth 7-8 in practical time. 5. The function handles the terminal conditions: winning, losing, draw (no valid moves), or depth limit reached. 6. Transposition table (not shown) could further optimize by storing previously evaluated positions.
examples.timeComplexity: O(b^(d/2)) with optimal move ordering; practical depth of 6-8 achievable in 5 seconds
examples.spaceComplexity: O(d) for recursion stack, can be enhanced with transposition table O(b*d)
